text: The surgeon performs sharp dissection in the surgical field without the use of electrocautery.
The surgical setup includes three robotic arms and a camera, all controlled by the robotic system, providing a precise view of the pelvic space during the prostatectomy.
The surgeon operates using robotic monopolar scissors in the right hand and a bipolar device in the left hand, manipulating tissues within the surgical field.
The surgeon utilizes the ProGrasp instrument with the robotic third arm to manipulate and apply counter-traction to the tissues during the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins the procedure with a posterior dissection, utilizing robotic instruments to carefully separate tissue layers.
The surgical team employs a transperitoneal approach, utilizing robotic arms and instruments to access the prostate area.
The surgeon employs the transperitoneal technique to precisely dissect the seminal vesicles and vasa, utilizing robotic instruments for enhanced accuracy.
The surgeon performs dissection of the seminal vesicles, revealing the presence of arteries supplying these structures.
The surgeon clips the arteries of the seminal vesicles, reducing the need for cautery near the lateral tissues where nerves responsible for sexual function are located.
The surgeon uses a nerve monitor from Propep Surgical to measure signals from the patient's neurovascular bundles, assessing their location and preservation during the prostatectomy.
The surgeon performs careful dissection lateral to the seminal vesicles, aiming to preserve the neurovascular tissue visible in the area to enhance the patient's postoperative recovery potential.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the seminal vesicles and begins focusing on Denonvilliers' fascia, preparing to manipulate this anatomical structure.
The surgeon identifies the membranous partition located at the lowest part of the rectovesicle pouch, highlighting its anatomical position within the surgical field.
The surgeon separates the prostate and urinary bladder from the rectum, visually delineating these anatomical structures during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the rectoprostatic fascia, a layer separating the prostate from the rectum, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses the left bipolar instrument to pull on the medial umbilical ligament, manipulating the structure within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses hot scissors to dissect tissue just lateral to the medial umbilical ligament, carefully separating the structures in this area.
The surgeon encounters a previously placed mesh that is positioned close to the dissection area, potentially hindering the surgical process.
The surgeon carefully navigates through the areolar tissue, attempting to maintain precision in the dissection area near the medial umbilical ligament.
The surgeon approaches the pubic symphysis, visible in the surgical field, as part of the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon locates the pubic bone, ensuring the dissection remains in a safe area to avoid damage to surrounding vasculature.
The surgeon mobilizes the anterior bladder wall, then identifies the endophilic fascia and removes the surrounding periprostatic fat from the prostate.
The surgeon completes the removal of the periprostatic fat surrounding the prostate, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The robotic system facilitates precise dissection along the levator muscles, allowing the surgeon to closely approach the dorsal vein.
The surgeon carefully spares individual muscle fibers during the robotic prostatectomy, a task that is visually precise and challenging compared to open surgery.
The surgeon carefully spares almost every muscle fiber during the dissection, preserving the integrity of the surrounding muscular tissue.
The surgeon performs cold dissection, carefully separating tissues without the use of heat-based instruments, minimizing the risk of thermal damage.
The surgeon operates on the left side of the prostate, cutting across the puboprostatic ligaments to expose the dorsal vein, with visible precision in the dissection process.
The surgeon uses robotic needle drivers to suture the dorsal vein, demonstrating precise control and manipulation of the needle and suture material.
The surgeon uses a zero PDS suture, likely to secure or ligate structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon demonstrates the use of a slip knot with a suture, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a suture to create a tight seal on the dorsal vein, minimizing potential bleeding during the prostatectomy procedure.
Following the removal of the prostate, the urethral angle visibly descends.
The surgeon performs a reconstruction to reposition the urethra to an anterior position, effectively securing it in place.
The surgeon prepares to begin the dissection of the bladder neck, positioning instruments to access and separate the bladder neck from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon introduces Propep electrodes into the surgical field, which will be used for nerve monitoring during the prostatectomy procedure.
The ProPep system is visibly integrated into the surgical robot, indicating its role in nerve monitoring during the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses the ProPep system to send a signal that stimulates the nerves surrounding the prostate, visible through the surgical robot interface.
Electrodes detect signals from nearby nerves, displaying a characteristic waveform on the monitor, indicating proximity to nerves associated with continence and sexual function.
The surgeon applies nerve stimulation around the prostate, observing the characteristic waveform signals that indicate the presence of nearby nerves involved in continence and sexual function.
The surgeon performs a dissection of the bladder neck, a challenging step in the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a fully inflated Foley balloon, moving it back and forth to visually identify the interface between the prostate and the bladder.
The surgeon carefully minimizes traction after opening the bladder neck, ensuring gentle handling of the surrounding tissues during the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon navigates through a loose areolar tissue plane, preparing to identify the vas deferens and seminal vesicles as part of the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon exposes the shiny white surface of the seminal vesicles and vasa, clearly visible within the areolar tissue plane.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the posterior bladder neck and begins focusing on the pedicles of the prostate, preparing for further surgical steps.
The surgeon prepares to initiate nerve-sparing techniques before dissecting the prostate pedicles, focusing on preserving the surrounding neural structures.
The surgeon performs both anti-grade and retrograde release of the neurovascular bundles, carefully manipulating these structures around the prostate.
The surgeon maintains a high position relative to the neurovascular bundles during the prostatectomy, ensuring careful navigation around these structures.
The surgeon utilizes nerve monitoring to confirm the tissue planes during the nerve-sparing portion of the prostatectomy.

The surgeon employs a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques, along with pinpoint cautery, to carefully release nerve tissues while maintaining a position high above the neurovascular bundles.
The surgeon approaches the apex of the prostate, maintaining proximity to the neurovascular bundles while performing the prostatectomy.
The site of the prior dorsal vein stitch is visible, indicating the location where the vein was previously sutured.
The surgeon applies cold irrigation to the surgical field, likely to clear debris or maintain tissue visibility during the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon completes the release of the neurovascular bundles and proceeds with posterior dissection in the area where the non-VA's fascia was previously opened.
The surgeon uses a sweeping motion to gently move the peri-rectal tissue and neurovascular bundles away from the prostate, creating a clear surgical field.
The surgeon applies a hemolog clip after safely removing the neurovascular bundles from the prostate during the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins to take the vascular pedicle, carefully isolating it from surrounding tissues during the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses cautery or a clip to secure the proximal part of the vascular pedicle, aiming to reduce any back bleeding during the procedure.
After clipping the vascular pedicle, the surgeon proceeds with the dissection of the nerve bundles, carefully separating them from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses pinpoint cautery and small clips to seal veins and small arteries, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical field.
The surgical team uses copious irrigation to minimize heat transfer during the procedure, ensuring the surgical field remains clear and reducing thermal damage to surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs dissection on the right side, using techniques similar to those previously applied, including cautery and clipping to manage vascular structures and minimize bleeding.
The surgeon releases the nerve bundles using both antegrade and retrograde techniques, carefully manipulating the tissue to free the nerves from surrounding structures.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the right posterior pedicle, adhering to the established principles of nerve preservation and careful tissue separation.
The surgeon prepares to complete the apical dissection, focusing on the precise separation of tissue at the apex of the prostate.
The surgeon uses cautery and sharp dissection in the region where the dorsal vein suture was initially applied, carefully manipulating the tissue in this area.
The surgical field is irrigated extensively, ensuring that the vascular bundles remain distant from the area of dissection.
The surgeon pinches the tissue in the area being dissected, applying a technique to facilitate the completion of the dissection.
The surgeon enters the space where the urethra lies, revealing areolar tissue surrounding the area.
The surgeon performs dissection of the urethra, focusing on this critical portion of the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon applies increased traction on the prostate, enhancing the visualization of the urethra as the dissection progresses.
The surgeon identifies the nerve bundles running just below the bipolar instrument, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a PDS suture on the dorsal vein, visible in the surgical field during the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgical field is irrigated by the assistant to maintain clarity, with minimal bleeding visible, ensuring accurate differentiation between prostate tissue and the urethra.
The surgeon begins using cautery in the operative field, with the neurovascular bundles visibly positioned outside the area of surgical activity.
The surgical field is relatively bloodless, allowing clear visualization that no prostate tissue extends underneath the urethra.
Following the removal of the prostate, the vascular bundles are visible on either side of the area where the prostate was previously located.
The urethra is visible as the neurovascular bundles converge just beneath it, with minimal bleeding in the surgical field.
The surgeon observes the contraction of the muscles surrounding the surgical site, highlighting the dynamic movement of the muscle fibers.
On a separate screen, the action potential is displayed as the nerve bundles are visually excited during the procedure.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis using two sutures in a Van Velthoven technique, connecting the urethra to the bladder neck following the prostate removal.
The surgeon uses monocryl sutures of different colors to distinguish between the left and right sides during the procedure.
The surgeon carefully manipulates tissues around the urethra, taking precautions to avoid damage to the urethra and surrounding nerve bundles during the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon demonstrates nerve-sparing technique, revealing the neurovascular bundles running and joining at the midline.
The surgeon performs the anastomosis in a running fashion, visually connecting the urethra to the bladder neck with continuous sutures.

The surgeon completes the final portion of the anastomosis and tests the Foley catheter by injecting at least 120 cc of sterile saline to check for leaks. The absence of leaks is visually confirmed, reducing the risk of urethral strictures.
The surgeon drops the pneumoperitoneum to visually inspect for any signs of bleeding within the surgical field.
The surgeon inspects the pouch of Douglas to ensure there are no visible injuries to the bowel or fluid collections, which may not be apparent during the surgery.
